description: Canonical adoption-layer model and risk-tier mapping for the cursor-guardrails template. Single source of truth for /guardrail-upgrade and downstream governance tools such as Throughline. Do not edit by hand — changes must be deliberate and version-bumped. Verified against the actual file tree by .github/scripts/check-manifest-drift.mjs in CI.

1.name: AI instructions
1.description: Rule files, slash commands, hooks, and cross-tool agent summary. Always safe to add — nothing here touches application code.

2.name: Git hygiene
2.description: Version control conventions, ignore rules, editor config, dependency management, legal, and contribution docs. Safe to add.

3.name: Commit discipline
3.description: Conventional Commits enforcement, pre-commit hooks, and lint-staged. Safe to add without touching application code.

4.name: Code quality toolchain
4.description: ESLint, Prettier, strict TypeScript. Merge carefully — may surface existing errors in application code.

5.name: CI pipeline
5.description: GitHub Actions: typecheck, lint, build, npm audit, secret scan (gitleaks), SAST (Semgrep). Merge carefully.

6.name: Code compliance audit
6.description: Manual audit of existing source code against installed rules. Classifies findings as Blocker / Should-fix / Nit. Not a file copy — a process step.

7.name: Governance activation
7.description: Human checklist: branch protection on main, Dependabot PRs reviewed, gh CLI installed. Not a file copy — a verification step.

Low.rationale: Low-risk initiatives require AI governance, version control discipline, and commit standards. Code quality and CI are strongly encouraged but not mandatory gates.

Medium.rationale: Medium-risk initiatives require full toolchain enforcement and a CI pipeline with security scanning in addition to the Low requirements.

High.rationale: High-risk initiatives require all of Medium plus a formal code compliance audit before build begins. Layer 7 governance activation is expected but recorded separately as a human checklist.

projectProfiles.description: Drives the Layer 0.4 'Project profile' step in guardrail-upgrade.md so the recommended layer set adapts to each project instead of a blanket 'apply all'. The risk question reuses riskTiers above — this block does not duplicate that mapping. Not every project has the same needs: a library needs different active domains than a full-stack app, and a low-risk prototype needs fewer required layers than a high-risk production service.

questions.prompt: What kind of project is this?
What is the risk level of this project?
Does the project already have its own ESLint/Prettier/tsconfig setup?
questions.effect: If yes, treat Layer 4 as extra merge-carefully: read both configs in full before adding strict settings, and never overwrite.

schemaChangelog.note: Initial publication. Encodes 7 adoption layers and Low/Medium/High tier mapping. Introduces 10 rule files including four new holistic domains (31-design, 32-ux-behavioural, 33-data-science, 50-ai-tooling).

Adds .cursor/mcp.json and .cursorignore to Layer 1 — both already existed in the template and were already listed in guardrail-upgrade.md, but had drifted out of this manifest. Adds the check-manifest-drift.mjs CI guard so Layer 1 cannot silently go out of date again.

Adds three new rule files to Layer 1 — 60-backend-api.mdc, 61-database.mdc, 62-deployment-observability.mdc — and three new domains (Backend, Database, Deployment), closing the gap where the template had no guidance once a project adds a server, database, or deployment pipeline. guardrailVersion patch bump per the new-rule-file convention.

Adds .cursor/commands/audit.md to Layer 1 — a new /audit command for whole-repo, point-in-time health audits (distinct from the diff-scoped /review and template-comparison /guardrail-upgrade). guardrailVersion patch bump per the new-file-in-Layer-1 convention established for rule files, extended here to command files.

Adds a new top-level projectProfiles block — the data model for the Layer 0.4 'Project profile' step in guardrail-upgrade.md. Maps project type to active vs inert-until-used domains and reuses the existing riskTiers block for the layer recommendation, so adoption is tailored per project instead of a blanket 'apply all'. Additive schema change (schemaVersion 2 -> 3); guardrailVersion patch bump (the system improved).
